text: history of present illness hpi narrative: uncontrolled blood pressure: - patient has been on amlodipine for many years for blood pressure control. - patient has been off the water pill for many months. - the physician plans to start the patient on lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide 10/5mg, to be taken in the morning. - the patient will also be provided with a blood pressure monitor to monitor her blood pressure at home. chronic back pain and left knee pain: - patient reports chronic back pain and pain in the left knee. - the patient is currently taking amlodipine for the pain. - the patient mentions a program called "silver sneakers" for exercise, which requires physician approval. - the physician plans to order x-rays for the lower back and left knee before clearing the patient for the exercise program.

text: 4. left knee pain imaging: left knee xray notes: [patient_name] [encounter_date] >[facility_name]: [address],[phone] notes: - chronic left knee pain noted; order x-rays of the left knee to assess for any underlying issues. - recommended conservative treatment with heat, ice therapy, and tylenol. - prescribe baclofen to help with pain and muscle relaxation, avoiding nsaids due to potential kidney impact and worsening blood pressure. - follow-up in three weeks to discuss x-ray results and further treatment options. 5. encounter to discuss test results notes: - reviewed lab results indicating no diabetes, anemia, or abnormalities in liver, electrolytes, or wbcs. - kidney function on the lower side but still within normal limits, likely due to long-standing hypertension. - provided a copy of the lab results for the patient to take home.

text: no heading found :: 6. others increase your need to use the restroom. - we will send a blood pressure monitor to your pharmacy. if it is not covered by insurance, please purchase one and monitor your blood pressure regularly. we discussed your lab results, including your a1c, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets, white blood cells, liver, electrolytes, and kidneys: - your a1c, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets, and white blood cells are all normal. - your liver and electrolytes are normal. - your kidneys are on the lower side of normal, which may be due to your long-term high blood pressure. we discussed your high cholesterol and triglycerides: - please follow a dash diet, which is low in salt and fat. - limit your intake of red meats, pork, fast foods, and pre-packaged foods. - increase your exercise, such as participating in the silver sneakers program. let us know if you need any forms filled out for this program. we discussed your chronic back pain and left knee pain: - please try over-the-counter tylenol for pain relief. - we will order x-rays of your lower back and left knee to further evaluate the cause of your pain. - you will need to call and make an appointment for the x-rays, or visit the location on parker street as a walk-in. we discussed your referrals to oncology, psychiatry, and neurology: - please follow up with the oncology specialist for your lung cancer treatment. - your appointment with the psychiatrist for post-traumatic stress disorder is scheduled for [encounter_date]. - please let us know if you receive a call from the neurologist. we discussed your mammogram: - we will provide you with another copy of the order for your mammogram. please schedule this appointment. we will see you in approximately three weeks to follow up on your blood pressure, x-rays, and any other concerns.

text: no heading found :: 6. others increase your need to use the restroom. - we will send a blood pressure monitor to your pharmacy. if it is not covered by insurance, please purchase one and monitor your blood pressure regularly. we discussed your lab results, including your a1c, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets, white blood cells, liver, electrolytes, and kidneys: - your a1c, hemoglobin, hematocrit, platelets, and white blood cells are all normal. - your liver and electrolytes are normal. - your kidneys are on the lower side of normal, which may be due to your long-term high blood pressure. we discussed your high cholesterol and triglycerides: - please follow a dash diet, which is low in salt and fat. - limit your intake of red meats, pork, fast foods, and pre-packaged foods. - increase your exercise, such as participating in the silver sneakers program. let us know if you need any forms filled out for this program. we discussed your chronic back pain and left knee pain: - please try over-the-counter tylenol for pain relief. - we will order x-rays of your lower back and left knee to further evaluate the cause of your pain. - you will need to call and make an appointment for the x-rays, or visit the location on parker street as a walk-in. we discussed your referrals to oncology, psychiatry, and neurology: - please follow up with the oncology specialist for your lung cancer treatment. - your appointment with the psychiatrist for post-traumatic stress disorder is scheduled for [encounter_date]. - please let us know if you receive a call from the neurologist. we discussed your mammogram: - we will provide you with another copy of the order for your mammogram. please schedule this appointment. we will see you in approximately three weeks to follow up on your blood pressure, x-rays, and any other concerns.

subject: PATIENT
heading: Preventive Medicine :: Anticipatory Guidance Well Visit:__page_no__16
text: preventive medicine anticipatory guidance well visit: immunizations - influenza, covid, tetanus (1 dose of tdap, then td or tdap booster every 10 years) hepatitis b, (in those with risk factors), recombinant zoster vaccine 2-6 months apart starting at age 50, pneumococcal (not previously received a dose of pcv13, pcv15, or pcv20 or whose previous vaccination history is unknown) 1 dose pcv15 or 1 dose pcv20, if pcv15 is used, this should be followed by a dose of ppsv23 given at least 1 year after the pcv15 dose. a minimum interval of 8 weeks between pcv15 and ppsv23 can be considered for adults with an immunocompromising condition, *cochlear implant or cerebrospinal fluid leak to minimize the risk of invasive pneumococcal disease caused by serotypes unique to ppsv23 in these vulnerable groups), the uspstf recommends screening for hepatitis b virus (hbv) infection in adolescents and adults at increased risk for infection., hepatitis c screening at least once in a lifetime for all adults aged 18 years and older, except in settings where the prevalence of hcv infection (hcv rna positivity) is less than 0.1%, the uspstf recommends screening for hepatitis b virus (hbv) infection in adults at increased risk for infection and hepatitis c screening at least once in a lifetime for all adults except (exclude settings where the prevalence of hcv is less than 0.1%).. screenings - the uspstf recommends biennial screening mammography for women aged 50-74, osteoporosis with bone measurements in women > 65+ (dexa), colorectal screening until age 75 cologuard or colonoscopy, (fit only if unable to perform

text: preventive medicine anticipatory guidance well visit: immunizations - influenza, covid, tetanus (1 dose of tdap, then td or tdap booster every 10 years) hepatitis b, (in those with risk factors), recombinant zoster vaccine 2-6 months apart starting at age 50, pneumococcal (not previously received a dose of pcv13, pcv15, or pcv20 or whose previous vaccination history is unknown) 1 dose pcv15 or 1 dose pcv20, if pcv15 is used, this should be followed by a dose of ppsv23 given at least 1 year after the pcv15 dose. a minimum interval of 8 weeks between pcv15 and ppsv23 can be considered for adults with an immunocompromising condition, *cochlear implant or cerebrospinal fluid leak to minimize the risk of invasive pneumococcal disease caused by serotypes unique to ppsv23 in these vulnerable groups), the uspstf recommends screening for hepatitis b virus (hbv) infection in adolescents and adults at increased risk for infection., hepatitis c screening at least once in a lifetime for all adults aged 18 years and older, except in settings where the prevalence of hcv infection (hcv rna positivity) is less than 0.1%, the uspstf recommends screening for hepatitis b virus (hbv) infection in adults at increased risk for infection and hepatitis c screening at least once in a lifetime for all adults except (exclude settings where the prevalence of hcv is less than 0.1%).. screenings - the uspstf recommends biennial screening mammography for women aged 50-74, osteoporosis with bone measurements in women > 65+ (dexa), colorectal screening until age 75 cologuard or colonoscopy, (fit only if unable to perform
